title: Lesson 367
transcript: Another exciting video here. We're still in domain number eight, which as you know is focused on, even though it's called software development security, it really should be called applications security. And what this section or module deals with, as you can see here, is how there needs to be some security in your development environment. So those environments where code is being produced need to have good security controls in place so that we can enforce, for example, secure coding guidelines, but also physical security controls and so on. That's what we're going to talk about here. in this section and we're going to start off with something you know really that's not going to be on the exam obviously but you know it highlights some of the problems that we've had in the past in fact you know you may have seen this diagram before that talks about the results of bad communication and you know how typically software development has gone in organizations because we typically start with the owners wanting something and you know then we send what are called business analysts to talk to the owners to try and understand what they want from a technology perspective and business analysts are supposed to be able to understand not only the business, but also technology so that they can relate the two together. But by the time we go back to technology and start talking about those things that the owners want, for example, we start losing things in translation. And that's what this is actually implying. the importance of involving everyone from the start and throughout is really what this highlights. In fact, when you look at this, I think Rob and I added one specific diagram in here or picture that you typically don't see and that's the one that says the security that was built in. which typically is nothing. Traditionally, security was never involved at the start, and we always had to retrofit security later into the entire process, which of course isn't cost-effective or efficient. What this slide talks about is the evolution that we've made, for example, in programming languages. So we've been programming computers ever since we invented them. there's a need to tell them what to do and that's what programs and code really does. And as you can see here, we started with machine language, binary basically that tells the computer what to do. zeros and ones and then we started to get better, better ways to allow programmers and developers to be able to tell the computer what to do. We moved into what's called assembler or assembly language and then we got even better with what's called structured languages or what some people refer to as high-level languages like COBOL. and PL1 and Fortran and RPG and some other things that are also considered to be structured languages. And really where we are today is in that next category that you see here. And that is referred to as object-oriented languages. And the key word there is the word object, which means a piece of code. And we can create pieces of code that provide some functionality that can be recalled and you know executed from other things and so on so any piece of code that does something functional or useful can be referred to as an object and you know what's really cool about the technologies that we have today and we'll talk about this later on is that we have complete tool sets that provide very good capabilities to be able to address not only functionality, but also security requirements, as you will see. All right, and the last category that you see their natural language programming is something that they've already made some cool headway into. It's like being able to speak instructions to a computer with structure and format of course, but the computer will be able to understand what you're saying and turn it into code automatically and execute that code on the fly, which is pretty cool evolution of obviously technology. But here's what we're going to talk about next, and certainly some of those evolutions that we have made in this area of software development includes what we call integrated development environments. IDE is what most people refer it to. And if you think about those three words, it tells you exactly what you've got here. All right, you've got an entire development environment that tries to automate and streamline a lot of the steps involved in not only coding software for example, but also compiling it and testing it and being able to put it into production. In fact, This is the basis for some of the other terminologies that are used today like CI-CD pipelines that we're going to talk about in a few minutes here. But obviously, these developments or these development environments that are integrated will include capabilities, entire tool sets to be able to allow developers to, for example, program their code and edit that code and then compile it and test it and debug and so on. The whole purpose of these environments is to automate as much as possible the entire process. so that it can be more streamlined and more cost effective, of course, to be able to do all of this. Now before we get to the CI-CD pipeline, One of the things that the exam may mention is that when we talked about those programming languages and the evolutions that we've gone through, you know, obviously we've come up with better ways to program applications, but the computer only understands one language because it's very efficient. And that is zeros and ones, binary or machine language. And so it doesn't matter what we program in, we have to turn it ultimately into machine language that the computer can understand. That's what these do. assembler will take or assemblers will take assembly language and turn it into binary code or machine language that the computer can execute. Compilers will take specifically high-level languages like COBOL and PL1 and so on, take that source code and turn it into object code that then can be run over and over again every time that application needs to be run. And that's the difference between compilers and interpreters because interpreters will take that source code and convert it into object code or machine language every time on the fly. For example, that's the difference between these. Now, here's something that's pretty important as far as the exam is concerned. And the industry refers to this as the CI-CD pipeline. And we're going to explain perfectly what this means from the perspective of those integrated development environments that we just talked about a couple of slides ago. So when we talk about the CI part, of CICD that always refers to continuous integration. And so if you look at, for example, the steps involved in allowing developers to develop code and then put it in a repository. And then, for example, being able to compile and test and do things like unit testing and integration testing and package testing. And then of course, once all of that is done, we need to obviously allow users to review and owners to accept and everything else. And then of course, once they do, we finally put things into production. Those are the steps involved in what they call the entire CI-CD pipeline. Automating some or all of these steps is what the CI-CD pipeline is all about so that we can streamline the entire process. So the CI part, continuous integration, what it does is it automates and streamlines what you see there right the developers being able to develop their code put it into a repository but also tries to integrate and automate the compiling and the testing that needs to be done at different levels so if you automate all of those steps It's what we refer to as continuous integration. Now the CD part of CICD can mean two things. It can mean continuous delivery, or continuous deployment. And here's the difference because there are two steps left here, obviously. So continuous delivery automates all of those steps up until the point where you allow, obviously, users and owners to be able to review things and acceptance. So acceptance testing is what that also adds to obviously continuous integration and continuous deployment automates and streamlines the entire thing, the entire process itself so that it becomes more efficient. And again, including security as part of all of this is a very important part of the CI-CD pipeline in a streamlined way. Now, the last item that we're going to talk about here in this section, actually two maybe, but this is called SOAR. It's something that was recently added into the exam outline. And as you can see, it actually refers to security orchestration, automation, and response. And what that means is trying to automate a lot of those tasks that typically we have done in a manual way that relate to security operations. Things like what you see here, doing threat and vulnerability management and being involved in that part of security operations. Participating in things like incident response, and doing things related to the day-to-day activities of security operations in your SOC centers and so on. Automating all of this and streamlining it is what SOAR is all about by using very intelligent capabilities and software that make this available. And here's the key point about this. Not only are we trying to get software to do all of this in a more intelligent way, but we're also trying to remove the human element from all of this. So think of very intelligent functional software that's driving all this, like machine learning and artificial intelligence, being able to do things that typically we needed the human element to do that are focused on security aspects. right, like what you see there. So allowing, you know, this very intelligent technology to be able to drive a lot of these things that are security related without involving the human element. Pretty exciting stuff, as you can see. And here's the last slide that we're gonna talk about here. And that is called software configuration management. And again, if you think about those words, it tells you exactly what this is all about. because ultimately applications have to run in some sort of environment that is configured properly the software itself the application itself has to be configured properly but so does the environment that it runs in being able to make the proper and required changes to that configuration is what this is all about. And trying to automate all of this as well is the whole purpose of software configuration management.
